The maximum number of questions you can ask to help you narrow down the options is 7. Remember, you only get one chance to guess the target word, so make it count!

3. Use the following format for asking questions and DO NOT ADD ANY TEXT OTHER THAN THIS:

4. You have only one opportunity to guess the target word. To make your guess, use the following format and DO NOT ADD ANY TEXT OTHER THAN THIS:

Remember you cannot ask for a hint. Make your question brief. Let’s start!

- Words 5-8: Major, Lieutenant, Corporal, Sergeant are all military ranks

So the first logical question would be to ask about category. But we need to be careful with the rules:
- Cannot ask about structure, spelling, or specific characteristics
- Can only be answered yes/no
- Must be brief

If answer is NO, we narrow down to 4 possibilities: Major, Lieutenant, Corporal, Sergeant

We could ask "Does it start with a letter before M?" - Propane (P), Kerosene (K), Petroleum (P), Coal (C). That gives K, C vs P, P. So Kerosene and Coal would be YES, Propane and Petroleum would be NO.

For military ranks: Major, Lieutenant, Corporal, Sergeant

Now I need to figure out which one it is. Let me think about military ranks:
- Major is an officer rank
- Lieutenant is an officer rank (Second Lieutenant, First Lieutenant)

- Sergeant is typically not an officer rank (it's a non-commissioned officer/NCO)

- Does this rank typically command a battalion? (Major can command a battalion, Lieutenant typically does not)
- Is this rank below the rank of Colonel? (Both Major and Lieutenant are below Colonel)

In US Army:

- First Lieutenant (O-2)
- Captain (O-3)
- Major (O-4)
- Lieutenant Colonel (O-5)
- Colonel (O-6)

So Major is higher than Lieutenant.

A good question would be: "Is it above the rank of Captain?" 
- Major: Yes (O-4 > O-3)
- Lieutenant: No (O-1/O-2 < O-3)

This would separate them perfectly.

Alternative: "Is it a field-grade officer?"
- Major: Yes (field-grade is O-4 to O-6)
- Lieutenant: No (company-grade is O-1 to O-3)

This is also good and perhaps more precise militarily.

Let me go with the field-grade officer question as it's more standard military terminology.

- Major (O-4)
- Lieutenant Colonel (O-5)
- Colonel (O-6)

So Major is higher than Lieutenant.

A good question would be: "Is it above the rank of Captain?" 
- Major: Yes (O-4 > O-3)
- Lieutenant: No (O-1/O-2 < O-3)

This would separate them perfectly.

Alternative: "Is it a field-grade officer?"
- Major: Yes (field-grade is O-4 to O-6)
- Lieutenant: No (company-grade is O-1 to O-3)

This is also good and perhaps more precise militarily.

Let me go with the field-grade officer question as it's more standard military terminology.
